title: 05_label_encoder_regression.ipynb
descriptionMd:
## Mission: Bring the Rent Estimator Back Under the Error Gate

The pricing team's rent estimator is deliberately small: read the listings table (city, square meters, bedrooms → monthly rent), turn the one categorical column into numbers, fit a linear regression, and keep validation MAE under the 150 EUR release gate.

Since the data-prep cell was tidied up, the gate is solidly red: MAE more than doubled to 312 EUR with the same data and the same model. Spot checks are stranger still — whole cities come out systematically over- or under-priced, as if the model had placed them on some invisible number line. Only one thing changed: how the `city` column becomes numbers. Work out what the encoder is really telling the regression.
initialCode:
import pandas as pd
from sklearn.linear_model import LinearRegression
from sklearn.metrics import mean_absolute_error
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
from sklearn.preprocessing import LabelEncoder

df = pd.read_csv("listings.csv")  # columns: city, sqm, bedrooms, rent
encoder = LabelEncoder()
df["city"] = encoder.fit_transform(df["city"])

X = df[["city", "sqm", "bedrooms"]]
y = df["rent"]
X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(
    X, y, test_size=0.2, random_state=7
)

model = LinearRegression().fit(X_train, y_train)
mae = mean_absolute_error(y_test, model.predict(X_test))
print(f"validation MAE: {mae:.0f} EUR")
assert mae < 150, f"MAE {mae:.0f} EUR exceeds the 150 EUR release gate"
traceback:

---------------------------------------------------------------------------

---> 20 assert mae < 150, f"MAE {mae:.0f} EUR exceeds the 150 EUR release gate"

AssertionError: MAE 312 EUR exceeds the 150 EUR release gate

options.label: One-hot encode the city column so each city gets its own independent coefficient
options.patchCode:
import pandas as pd
from sklearn.linear_model import LinearRegression
from sklearn.metrics import mean_absolute_error
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split

df = pd.read_csv("listings.csv")  # columns: city, sqm, bedrooms, rent
df = pd.get_dummies(df, columns=["city"])

X = df.drop(columns=["rent"])
y = df["rent"]
X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(
    X, y, test_size=0.2, random_state=7
)

model = LinearRegression().fit(X_train, y_train)
mae = mean_absolute_error(y_test, model.predict(X_test))
print(f"validation MAE: {mae:.0f} EUR")
assert mae < 150, f"MAE {mae:.0f} EUR exceeds the 150 EUR release gate"
options.rationale: `pd.get_dummies` expands `city` into one 0/1 column per city, so the regression learns an independent rent offset for each one — no order, no spacing, no claim that cairo is 'twice' boston. That matches what `city` actually is: a nominal category. Integer codes are for the target column or for genuinely ordinal features; nominal features get one column per category (or target/embedding encodings once cardinality explodes).

options.label: Switch from LabelEncoder to OrdinalEncoder, the feature-encoding API the docs point to
options.patchCode:
import pandas as pd
from sklearn.linear_model import LinearRegression
from sklearn.metrics import mean_absolute_error
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
from sklearn.preprocessing import OrdinalEncoder

df = pd.read_csv("listings.csv")  # columns: city, sqm, bedrooms, rent
encoder = OrdinalEncoder()
df[["city"]] = encoder.fit_transform(df[["city"]])

X = df[["city", "sqm", "bedrooms"]]
y = df["rent"]
X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(
    X, y, test_size=0.2, random_state=7
)

model = LinearRegression().fit(X_train, y_train)
mae = mean_absolute_error(y_test, model.predict(X_test))
print(f"validation MAE: {mae:.0f} EUR")
assert mae < 150, f"MAE {mae:.0f} EUR exceeds the 150 EUR release gate"
options.resultLog:
validation MAE: 312 EUR
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
AssertionError                            Traceback (most recent call last)
Cell In[1], line 20
     18 mae = mean_absolute_error(y_test, model.predict(X_test))
     19 print(f"validation MAE: {mae:.0f} EUR")
---> 20 assert mae < 150, f"MAE {mae:.0f} EUR exceeds the 150 EUR release gate"

AssertionError: MAE 312 EUR exceeds the 150 EUR release gate
options.rationale: It looks like the by-the-book fix — sklearn's docs really do say LabelEncoder is for targets and OrdinalEncoder is for features — but that warning is about API shape, not about ordinality. `OrdinalEncoder`'s default categories are sorted alphabetically too, so it emits bit-for-bit the same integer codes, the model fits the same fake number line, and the MAE lands on exactly the same 312. The problem was never which class assigned the integers; it's that one integer column asserts an order cities don't have.

options.label: Drop the city column entirely — free-text strings don't belong in a regression
options.patchCode:
import pandas as pd
from sklearn.linear_model import LinearRegression
from sklearn.metrics import mean_absolute_error
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split

df = pd.read_csv("listings.csv")  # columns: city, sqm, bedrooms, rent

X = df[["sqm", "bedrooms"]]
y = df["rent"]
X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(
    X, y, test_size=0.2, random_state=7
)

model = LinearRegression().fit(X_train, y_train)
mae = mean_absolute_error(y_test, model.predict(X_test))
print(f"validation MAE: {mae:.0f} EUR")
assert mae < 150, f"MAE {mae:.0f} EUR exceeds the 150 EUR release gate"

options.rationale: This cures the fake ordering by amputating the strongest predictor in the table. Location dominates rent — the same 70 m² flat differs by hundreds of euros between cities — so without `city` the model can only fit one pan-European price per square meter, and MAE gets worse than the bug it was meant to fix. A feature being categorical means it needs the right encoding, not deletion.

hints: A linear regression can only do one thing with a feature: multiply it by a single coefficient. The moment a category becomes one number, arithmetic claims appear out of thin air — is one city now twice another? Is the gap between two cities a measurable quantity the model should extrapolate?

Lines 8–9: `LabelEncoder.fit_transform` numbers the cities alphabetically — amsterdam=0, boston=1, cairo=2 — and sklearn reserves that class for encoding *targets*. But swapping in a different encoder class changes nothing while `city` stays a single integer column claiming an order that isn't real.
explanationMd:
### Why the bug occurs
`LabelEncoder` assigns integers in sorted order: amsterdam→0, boston→1, cairo→2, and so on down the alphabet. `LinearRegression` must then express the city's effect as one coefficient times that code, which forces it to claim rent changes by a fixed amount per alphabetical step — that cairo sits exactly twice as far from amsterdam as boston does, and that the jump from helsinki to lisbon equals the jump from lisbon to madrid. No such structure exists in the data, so the coefficient settles on a meaningless compromise and whole cities come out systematically mispriced. Nothing throws: the integer codes are perfectly legal inputs, so the only symptom is the red MAE gate.

### Why the fix is correct
One-hot encoding (`pd.get_dummies` here, `OneHotEncoder` inside a pipeline) gives every city its own 0/1 column and therefore its own independently learned coefficient — a per-city rent offset with no ordering or spacing imposed between categories. That matches the variable's measurement scale: `city` is *nominal*. The rule of thumb: single integer codes are for the target, or for genuinely ordinal features whose order you state explicitly; nominal features get one column per category, or target/embedding encodings once cardinality gets large.
recruiterReview: Correct fix, and you diagnosed the real issue: the encoder wasn't malfunctioning, it was making a claim — integer codes assert an order and a spacing, and a linear model will faithfully learn whatever your preprocessing asserts. Matching the encoding to the feature's measurement scale (nominal vs ordinal) is the principle here, and I'm glad you didn't take the OrdinalEncoder bait; same integers, same bug, friendlier docstring. One forward-looking note: move the encoding into a ColumnTransformer with OneHotEncoder(handle_unknown="ignore") inside the model pipeline, so the category vocabulary is frozen at fit time and a brand-new city at serving time degrades gracefully instead of crashing the scorer. Approved. ✅

tutorial.bodyMd:
## Encoding Categories: The Numbers You Pick Are Claims You Make

Models do arithmetic. The moment a category becomes a number, every arithmetic property of that number becomes a statement about the category: 2 is bigger than 1, twice as big, and exactly as far from 1 as 1 is from 0. Choosing an encoding is therefore not plumbing — it is the first modeling decision in the pipeline, and the variable's measurement scale decides it.

### Nominal vs ordinal
A **nominal** variable is a set of labels with no order: city, color, payment method, product category. An **ordinal** variable has a genuine ranking but unknown spacing: shirt sizes, satisfaction grades, education levels. Only ordinal variables can tolerate a single integer column, and even then you must hand the encoder the true order explicitly — `OrdinalEncoder(categories=[["S", "M", "L", "XL"]])` — because the alphabetical default is almost never the real ranking.

### What integer codes do to different models
Linear and distance-based models (linear/logistic regression, SVMs, kNN, neural nets) consume codes as magnitudes. One coefficient per column means "the outcome rises by β per unit of city", and kNN will happily decide city 3 is closer to city 4 than to city 9. Tree-based models are more forgiving: a tree can carve an integer axis into arbitrary subsets given enough splits, which is exactly why this bug hides inside a random-forest baseline for months and only detonates when someone swaps in a linear model and the metric collapses.

### The standard tools
- **One-hot** (`OneHotEncoder`, `pd.get_dummies`): one 0/1 column per category, each with its own independent weight. The default for nominal features at modest cardinality.
- **Ordinal with an explicit order**: for genuinely ranked categories — pass the ordering yourself, never trust the sorted default.
- **Target/impact encoding or learned embeddings**: for high cardinality (thousands of ZIP codes or SKUs), fitted with cross-validation so the target doesn't leak into the features.
- **`LabelEncoder`**: built for the *y* column. It exists so classifiers can map class names to integers, where order is harmless because nothing ever multiplies the codes.

### Production guardrails
Fit encoders on training data only, and keep them inside a `Pipeline`/`ColumnTransformer` so the category vocabulary is frozen with the model artifact. Set `OneHotEncoder(handle_unknown="ignore")` so an unseen category at serving time becomes an all-zeros row instead of an exception. For unregularized linear models with an intercept, `drop="first"` removes the redundant dummy column. And keep a baseline metric in CI: encoding bugs rarely throw — they just quietly cost you accuracy, and only a gate notices.

### Common variations of this bug
- `OrdinalEncoder` (or a hand-rolled `{category: i}` dict) on a nominal feature — the identical fake number line under a doc-approved name.
- Alphabetical integer codes on a *genuinely ordinal* feature, scrambling the true order ("high" < "low" < "medium").
- ZIP codes, user IDs, or product IDs fed in as raw integers because they already look numeric.
- One-hot columns fit separately on train and test (or refit at serving), so the column layout silently disagrees with what the model learned.
- Target encoding computed over the full dataset, leaking label information into the features and inflating offline metrics.
